cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60-year-old presents to ED unwell with tachypnoeic, hypotensive, and with pneumonia.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60-year-old presents to ED unwell with febrile, hypotensive, and hypotensive.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old presents dehydrated and weak after several days of persistent upper gastrointestinal losses.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~49.1; measured 49.7 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old presents to ED with abdominal pain, with deep rapid breathing, and after missed insulin doses.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 32-year-old is brought in drowsy with bradypnoea and decreased responsiveness.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.4; measured 25.8 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents confused, tachypnoeic, and febrile with ringing in the ears.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (138 âˆ’ (96 + 15) = 27) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 26-year-old presents with acute dyspnoea, dizziness, and hand paraesthesiae with a normal cardiorespiratory examination.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~20.8; measured 21.0 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winterâ€™s formula predicts PaCO2 ~27.5 (Â±2); measured 28.5 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 137 âˆ’ (94 + 13) = 30 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (136 âˆ’ (97 + 15) = 24) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60-year-old is brought in unwell with tachycardic, with suspected sepsis, and with rigors.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old with chronic respiratory symptoms presents with worsening shortness of breath over several days.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~36.4; measured 37.0 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old presents to ED febrile, with confusion, and hypotensive.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 35-year-old presents with weakness and dehydration after ongoing gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~46.3; measured 46.2 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 30-year-old presents with rapid breathing, light-headedness, and perioral tingling after an acute stressful event.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.6; measured 21.5 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.
